title: Orgology

content.statement: A company is a system of structures, incentives, and feedback, design it consciously from day one.
content.question: What structural element of your company is producing behavior you dislike, and would redesigning it work better than scolding people?
content.equation: Organizational Health = Structure × Incentives × Feedback Quality × Cultural Coherence
content.mental_model: Organism, not a machine, health depends on internal coordination.
content.business_lens: Design org structure, decision rights, and feedback rhythms intentionally; revisit them at each scale milestone.
content.life_lens: Notice the structures around you that shape behavior, and design yours intentionally rather than by default.
sop: Map the current structure: roles, decision rights, feedback flows
Redesign one element at a time
Codify simple rituals (goal reviews, retros, decision logs)
content.explainer: Orgology treats the company as a designed system. The structures and incentives produce most of the behavior; blaming individuals is rarely the highest-leverage move. Beer’s VSM and modern org-design literature emphasize feedback loops as the load-bearing element.
questions: What structural element of your company is producing behavior you dislike, and would redesigning it work better than scolding people?
Which recurring complaint about a person on your team would actually disappear if you changed the process around them instead?
What ritual, or lack of one, are you running today that only worked because your team was three people, not ten?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Structure 6, Incentives 7, Feedback Quality 5, Cultural Coherence 8. Score = (6 plus 7 plus 5 plus 8) divided by 4 = 6.5. Higher means your org design is actively producing the behavior you want; lower means you're relying on individual willpower to compensate for structural gaps.
content.closing_statement: Structure produces behavior faster than culture talks about it.

caption:
Principle Nº 311: Orgology

A company is a system of structures, incentives, and feedback, design it consciously from day one. Orgology treats the company as a designed system.

Ask yourself: What structural element of your company is producing behavior you dislike, and would redesigning it work better than scolding people?
